revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:藤原四兄弟]][[zh:藤原四兄弟]]
'''Fujiwara Four Brothers''' (藤原四兄弟, Fujiwara shikyōdai or Fujiwara yonkyōdai) is a historical term referring to the four sons of {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|qid=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito|1=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}, who held power during the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō era|1=Tenpyō}} in the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. They are also known as '''Fujiwara Shishi''' (藤原四子). == The Fujiwara Four Brothers ==
* {{ill|Fujiwara no Muchimaro|en|Fujiwara no Muchimaro|qid=Q1890878|lt=Fujiwara no Muchimaro|1=Fujiwara no Muchimaro}} (680–737) - Founder of the {{ill|Fujiwara Nan-ke|en|Fujiwara Nan-ke|qid=Q2975332|lt=Fujiwara South House|1=Fujiwara Nan-ke}} * {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|en|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|qid=Q2173602|lt=Fujiwara no Fusasaki|1=Fujiwara no Fusasaki}} (681–737) - Founder of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|qid=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara North House|1=Fujiwara Hok-ke}} * {{ill|Fujiwara no Umakai|en|Fujiwara no Umakai|qid=Q1370638|lt=Fujiwara no Umakai|1=Fujiwara no Umakai}} (694–737) - Founder of the {{ill|Fujiwara Shiki-ke|en|Fujiwara Shiki-ke|qid=Q2975364|lt=Fujiwara Ceremonial House|1=Fujiwara Shiki-ke}} * {{ill|Fujiwara no Maro|en|Fujiwara no Maro|qid=Q426317|lt=Fujiwara no Maro|1=Fujiwara no Maro}} (695–737) - Founder of the {{ill|Fujiwara Kyō-ke|en|Fujiwara Kyō-ke|qid=Q2975302|lt=Fujiwara Capital House|1=Fujiwara Kyō-ke}}
Muchimaro, Fusasaki, and Umakai were full brothers, while Maro was their half-brother.<ref group="note">Some theories suggest that Umakai was not a full brother to Muchimaro and Fusasaki due to the gap in birth years.</ref> Additionally, {{ill|Fujiwara no Miyako|ja|藤原宮子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Miyako|1=Fujiwara no Miyako|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11623185}} (mother of Emperor Shōmu) and {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|qid=Q837932|lt=Empress Kōmyō|1=Empress Kōmyō}} (Fujiwara no Kōmyōshi) were their half-sisters.
== Fujiwara Shishi Regime ==
After Fuhito's death, the four brothers competed for power with {{ill|Prince Nagaya|en|Prince Nagaya|qid=Q1963492|lt=Prince Nagaya|1=Prince Nagaya}} during the reigns of {{ill|Empress Genshō|en|Empress Genshō|qid=Q235343|lt=Empress Genshō|1=Empress Genshō}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}. Following the {{ill|Incident of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Incident of Prince Nagaya|1=Prince Nagaya incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109357375}}, the brothers consolidated power. Muchimaro (as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}) and Fusasaki (as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}) were joined by Umakai and Maro, who were promoted to Sangi, thus occupying four out of nine seats in the Council of State from 729 to 737. This period is known as the '''Fujiwara Shishi Regime'''.
During this time, fiscal reforms were implemented, including the establishment of mixed official grain taxation in 734 and the capital land tax in 736. Administrative reforms introduced regional governors and strengthened security through new posts such as {{ill|Chinbusho|ja|鎮撫使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbusho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and later {{ill|Jiedushi|en|Jiedushi|qid=Q1688932|lt=Settoshi|1=Jiedushi}}. International diplomacy included envoys to Silla and military campaigns in the northeast.
However, in 737, the {{ill|735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic|en|735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic|qid=Q60752789|lt=smallpox epidemic|1=735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic}} claimed the lives of all four brothers, marking the end of their regime. The sudden deaths were seen as divine retribution from Prince Nagaya's spirit.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://president.jp/articles/-/68743?page=4 |title=The Worst Pandemic in Ancient Japan's History |publisher=President Online |date=2023-04-25 |accessdate=2023-04-25}}</ref>
With their children still young, power shifted to {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|en|Tachibana no Moroe|qid=Q3135863|lt=Tachibana no Moroe|1=Tachibana no Moroe}}, the maternal half-brother of Empress Kōmyō, who became {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|qid=Q1351174|lt=Udaijin|1=Minister of the Right}}. Later, the Fujiwara family's influence reemerged through the rise of Muchimaro's son {{ill|Fujiwara no Toyonari|en|Fujiwara no Toyonari|qid=Q3090678|lt=Fujiwara no Toyonari|1=Fujiwara no Toyonari}} and then {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|qid=Q540305|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro}} during the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|qid=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}}.
The descendants of the four brothers formed the {{ill|Fujiwara Nan-ke|en|Fujiwara Nan-ke|qid=Q2975332|lt=Fujiwara South House|1=Fujiwara Nan-ke}}, {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|qid=Q2975268|lt=North House|1=Fujiwara Hok-ke}}, {{ill|Fujiwara Shiki-ke|en|Fujiwara Shiki-ke|qid=Q2975364|lt=Ceremonial House|1=Fujiwara Shiki-ke}}, and {{ill|Fujiwara Kyō-ke|en|Fujiwara Kyō-ke|qid=Q2975302|lt=Capital House|1=Fujiwara Kyō-ke}}. While each lineage experienced periods of prosperity and decline, they all left lasting impacts on Japanese politics, scholarship, and culture. See also {{ill|Fujiwara Four Houses|ja|藤原四家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Four Houses|1=Fujiwara Four Houses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3704262}}.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
* Nakagawa Osamu, "The Structure and Characteristics of the Fujiwara Shishi System," in ''Nihon Rekishi'' No. 320, 1975. * Watanabe Akihiro, ''The Nara Capital and the Age of Wooden Tablets'' (Kodansha, 2001). * Kimoto Yoshinobu, ''Fujiwara Four Brothers'' (Minerva Shobo, 2013).
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Fujiwara Four Houses|ja|藤原四家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Four Houses|1=Fujiwara Four Houses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3704262}} ]]]]]]
